title: The wealthier the country, the lower the NEET
subtitle: Comparing NEET rates with GDP per capita
source: WB, World Development Indicators (SL.UEM.NEET.ZS)
y_title: Share of youth not in education, employment or training

scenes.text: The share of youth (15-24 years of age) not currently in employment, education or training (NEET) is correlated with the income level of the country in which they live. NEET differs greatly across regions with the highest rates observed in Middle East & North Africa, Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. [This regional pattern mimics that of labor force participation.]
In South Asia levels are on average quite high.
In the Middle East & North Africa levels are on average quite high, but there is a strong negative correlation between NEET and GDP even within the region.
Sub-Saharan Africa has the highest levels of NEET on average of any region, without any clear relationship with a country’s income level.
Similar to the trends observed regarding labor force participation in [goal: 5] these regional differences tell a striking story related to gender differences.

Generally, women are more likely to be outside employment, education or training. And in some regions, these differences across genders are substantial. Here, each orange line connecting an orange and purple dot shows the NEET for an individual country. The longer the line, the greater the difference between NEET rates across gender. Generally, countries with a lower income level tend to have a greater divergence in NEET rates across gender, although regional differences are substantial.
In South Asia, for instance, India stands out by having NEET rates for women almost 4 times the NEET rate for men.

In the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan region, approximately 1 in 3 youth are not in employment, education or training and the share of females in NEET is almost twice that of men. These significant differences in NEET rates across gender persist across different income levels.
While Sub-Saharan Africa has the highest level of NEET across regions, gender differences are less profound although a few countries exhibit significant differences in NEET across men and women.
